results.possibleIdentity:
Morton G. Wurtele (1919–2004) was a prominent American meteorologist and professor of atmospheric science at the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA). Born in Harrodsburg, Kentucky, he earned a mathematics degree from Harvard (1940) before serving as a weather forecaster during World War II. He received his PhD from UCLA in 1953 and, after a brief tenure at MIT, returned to UCLA in 1958, eventually serving as the chairman of the Department of Meteorology from 1971 to 1976. 

Wurtele's research spanned various technical fields, including atmospheric dynamics, mountain waves (as a key participant in the Sierra Wave Project), clear-air turbulence, and the development of numerical methods for atmospheric modeling. He was a pioneer in remote sensing and satellite photography, collaborating with NASA and the Jet Propulsion Laboratory on satellite microwave scatterometers (such as the SEASAT-A system) to map ocean winds. His technical expertise also extended to radar tracking, electronic computation, and 'whole-sky' photography (all-sky camera techniques for cloud study). 

Beyond his scientific career, Wurtele was known for his interdisciplinary interests and involvement in the arts. While a student in Santa Monica during the 1940s, he was a close friend and scientific advisor to playwright Bertolt Brecht, assisting with the technical and scientific aspects of the English translation and production of the play 'Galileo'. He was also an enthusiast of music and gourmet cooking. Given his multi-faceted background and specific technical skills (balloons, radar, satellite photography), he was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s and early 1970s, a period when he was active in Santa Monica and UCLA.
